State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administrate: • • • Icecitus/legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 327-5632           32-5660            322-5901           327-6441        122-6441

June 27, 1979

Honorable John A. Nejedly
Member, California Senator
400 Montecillo Drive
Walnut Creek, CA 94595

Dear Senator Nejedly:

You have asked about the reportability of donations that will be made to construct certain facilities on property that you own. The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: You own land in Sierra County which is leased to Contra Costa County for a summer camp. Because the land is inaccessible most of the year, it is used only for the county camp, in order to accommodate handicapped children at the summer camp, some additional facilities will be constructed. Private parties will donate some of the materials necessary for construction of those facilities. The lease between you and Contra Costa County provides that any increase in the value of the land arising from the construction of any facilities will be paid to the County upon termination of the lease.

Government Code Section 82028_1/ defines a gift as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." In the circumstances you have outlined, we believe that the provision of the lease requiring you to pay the increased value of the improvements to the County and the fact that the land is used only for the summer camp prevents the donations from being gifts to you. The lease provision insures that you will provide full consideration for the residual value of the improvements at the end of the lease. And the fact that the property is used for the summer camp indicates that you will derive no financial benefit from the improvements during the term of the lease. Thus, you will pay full and adequate consideration for any benefit you derive from the donations and will not receive a gift.

_________________________

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code.

Honorable John A. Nejedly
June 27, 1979
Page Two


     The fact that payment is made to the County rather than
the original donors of the improvements does not alter this
conclusion. There is no requirement in the law that the
consideration be paid to the person who has provided the
goods or services in return. Such a requirement would un-
necessarily convert many normal quid pro quo financial trans-
actions (such as bank credit card purchases) from sales to
gifts.

     The donations also cannot be considered income to you.
In these circumstances you will purchase the improvements
that will be constructed at fair market value. The purchase
of goods or services in situations such as this one does not
result in income to the purchaser.

     Because the donations will not be either gifts or income
to you, you have no obligation to report them on your Statement
of Economic Interest.

     If the facilities constructed increase the fair market
value of the real property from one reporting category into
another (i.e., from "under $10,000," to "over $10,000, but
not over $100,000"), your Statement of Economic Interest
should reflect that increase.

     If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Lee C. Rosenthal
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

LCS:kp

John A. Nejedly
400 Montecillo Drive
Walnut Creek, Ca. 94595

March 16, 1979


Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

As the previous communications to and actions by the Commission
have indicated, I am a member of the Board of Directors of the
Contra Costa County Youth Association, and contributions to that
non-profit charitable association operate a summer camp program in
Sierra County on property I purchased for that purpose in 1963.

The Commission has previously understood that contributions to
that program are not related to my reporting to the Commission.
However, incident to the expansion of our program to accommodate
Easter Seal and Handicapped Children, new sophisticated structures
and facilities are now required and, which, to some extent, may add
to the value of the property. To provide for that possibility, the
lease with Contra Costa County has been amended to provide that any
such increase, if it occurs, should be paid by myself or my successors to the County of Contra Costa. I enclose a copy of that lease.

We will, this coming season, complete a handicapped childrens'
toilet facility, a water system for that use, relocate the cooking
facility and provide a permanent building with sleeping facilities
and accommodations during inclement weather.

We will be receiving materials for these purposes as donations.

Will you be kind enough to indicate if such donations should be reported to you and in what manner the reporting should be made?

Very truly yours,

JOHN A. NEJEDLY

Enclosure

In the Board of Supervisors
of
Contra Costa County, State of California

December 5                    , 19 78


In the Matter of

Authorizing Execution of a Lease
with John A. Nejedly for the prem-
ises at Hawley Lake, Sierra County




IT IS BY THE BOARD ORDERED that the Chairman of the Board of
Supervisors is AUTHORIZED to execute on behalf of the County a lease commencing
July 1, 1979 with John A. Nejedly for the premises at Hawley Lake, Sierra County,
for use by the Probation and Social Service Departments.


PASSED by this Board on December 5, 1978                    .

LEASE

Hawley Lake
Sierra County, California


1. PARTIES: Effective on ____December 5, 1978__, JOHN A NEJEDLY, hereinafter called
"LESSOR", and the COUNTY OF CONTRA COSTA, a political subdivision of the State of Calif
ornia, hereinafter called "COUNTY", mutually agree and promise as follows:

2. LEASE OF PREMISES: LESSOR, in consideration of the rents specified below, hereby
leases to COUNTY, and COUNTY accepts and takes those certain premises known as Hawley L
in Sierra County, California, more particularly described in Exhibit "A" attached here
and made a part hereof.

3. RENTAL: COUNTY shall pay to LESSOR as rent for use of said premises a yearly rentａ
One and No/100 Dollars ($1.00) all payable in advance at the time of execution of this
lease. Payment shall be mailed to LESSOR at 400 Monticello Drive, Walnut Creek, CA 94:

4. TERM: The term of this lease is five (5) years commencing July 1, 1979 and terminａ
June 30, 1984.

5. EXTENSION: COUNTY shall have the option of renewing this lease on the same terms ａ
conditions for an additional five (5) years provided that it notifies LESSOR of its deａ
sion to renew no later than ninety (90) days prior to the above-mentioned termination
This lease may be terminated by the COUNTY at any time by giving written notice to the

the maintenance and repair of [illegible] structures and facilities [illegible] ated on the leased premise

9. HOLD HARMLESS: It is understood and agreed that the LESSOR shall not in any way be
( responsible for damages to persons or property in or upon said premises during its use by
the COUNTY each year, and shall not be held liable for any liability, claim or suit for
damages to the person or property of anyone whomsoever while in or upon said leased premi
during said period of COUNTY use, and the COUNTY hereby agrees to indemnify and hold harm
less the LESSOR from any liability or charges of any kind or character by reason of such
injury or damage claim or suit for liability arising therefrom in or around said premises
during said period of COUNTY use, except that LESSOR shall be held liable for damage to
persons or property in or upon the leased premises resulting from his sole negligence.

10. QUIET ENJOYMENT: LESSOR covenants that COUNTY shall at all times during the said ter
peaceably and quietly have, hold and enjoy the demised premises without suit, trouble or
hindrance from or on account of LESSOR as long as COUNTY fully performs hereunder.

11. DEFAULTS: In the event of COUNTY breach of any of the covenants or conditions herei
including rent payment, LESSOR may re-enter and repossess the premises and remove all per
sons and property therefrom; and in the event of such a breach by LESSOR, COUNTY may quit
the premises with no further obligation under this lease.

12. SURRENDER OF PREMISES: On the last day of the said term, COUNTY will peaceably and
quietly leave and surrender to LESSOR these premises with their appurtenances and fixture
in a condition equal to that which existed at the time COUNTY began its last period of us
of the property, reasonable use and wear thereof and damage by earthquake, fire, public
[illegible] by Act of God or by circumstances over which the COUNTY has no

15. TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE of each and all of the terms and provisions of this lease.

COUNTY                                           LESSOR

COUNTY OF CONTRA COSTA, a
political subdivision of the
State of California                              JOHN A. NEJEDLY

By ________________________                By _______________________
   Chairman, Board of Supervisors
   R.I Schroder

ATTEST: J. R. OLSSON, Clerk

By _______________________________
   Deputy     Karin King

RECOMMENDED FOR APPROVAL:

By _______________________________
   County Administrator

By _______________________________
   Deputy Public Works Director
   Buildings and Grounds

By _______________________________

embedding.qa_text: June 27, 1979 Honorable John A. Nejedly Member, California Senator 400 Montecillo Drive Walnut Creek, CA 94595 Dear Senator Nejedly: You have asked about the reportability of donations that will be made to construct certain facilities on property that you own. The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: You own land in Sierra County which is leased to Contra Costa County for a summer camp. Because the land is inaccessible most of the year, it is used only for the County camp. In order to accommodate handicapped children at the summer camp, some additional facilities will be constructed. Private parties will donate some of the materials necessary for construction of those facilities. The lease between you and Contra Costa County provides that any increase in the value of the land arising from the construction of any facilities will be paid to the County upon termination of the lease. Government Code Section 82028 1/ defines a gift as "any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." In the circumstances you have outlined, we believe that the provision of the lease requiring you to pay the increased value of the improvements to the County and the fact that the land is used only for the summer camp prevents the donations from being gifts to you. The lease provision insures that you will provide full consideration for the residual value of the improvements at the end of the lease. And the fact that the property is used only for the summer camp indicates that you will derive no financial benefit from the improvements during the term of the lease. Thus, you will pay full and adequate consideration for any benefit you derive from the donations and will not receive a gift. 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code. The fact that payment is made to the County rather than the original donors of the improvements does not alter this conclusion. There is no requirement in the law that the consideration be paid to the person who has provided the goods or services in return. Such a requirement would unnecessarily convert many normal quid pro quo financial transactions (such as bank credit card purchases) from sales to gifts. The donations also cannot be considered income to you. In these circumstances you will purchase the improvements that will be constructed at fair market value. The purchase of goods or services in situations such as this one does not result in income to the purchaser. Because the donations will not be either gifts or income to you, you have no obligation to report them on your Statement of Economic Interest. If the facilities constructed increase the fair market value of the real property from one reporting category into another (i.e., from "under $10,000," to "over $10,000, but not over $100,000"), your Statement of Economic Interest should reflect that increase. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me. Sincerely, Lee C.
